result:
I sit in a small café by a window that looks out onto a street I have never walked. The glass is smudged with the ghosts of old rain and the fingerprints of strangers. Outside, a woman in a blue coat stops to adjust her scarf. She does not know I am watching her, and in that moment, I love her. I love her for the simple, unguarded act of twisting wool around her neck. I love her because she is a mystery I will never solve. A man walks past with a dog that sniffs every lamppost with the desperate seriousness of a philosopher. The man’s phone rings, and he laughs at something on the screen, and I wonder if it is genuine or the polite chuckle we all learn to use in public. Out here, everyone is an actor on a stage they didn’t choose.

Inside the café, the air smells of roasted beans and ambition. A student at the table next to me is writing in a notebook with the furious intensity of someone trying to capture a thought before it evaporates. Her pen scratches like a conscience. She pauses, bites her lip, and underlines a word three times. I want to know what it says, but I also want to preserve the sacred distance between observer and observed. There is a strange intimacy in a public space. We sit inches apart, yet we are galaxies. We share the same air, the same warmth, the same silence, but we will never speak. I wonder if she feels the same loneliness I feel, the same weight of the unexpressed.

The barista calls out a name. “Cortado for Sarah.” Sarah stands up. She is not the student. She is an older woman with silver hair and reading glasses on a chain. She takes her tiny cup with both hands, as if it is a holy relic. She returns to her table and pulls out a thick novel with a creased spine. I watch her turn a page. Her eyes move slowly, savouring each sentence. I imagine she has lived a full life. Perhaps she was a teacher. Perhaps she lost someone she loved. Perhaps she is escaping into this book the way I am escaping into this moment, into the lives of strangers.

My coffee is growing cold. I take a sip, and it tastes of bitterness and memory. I think about how we are all just stories waiting to be told, and how most of them will never be told. The man with the dog is gone now. The woman in the blue coat has disappeared into the crowd. The student finally closes her notebook and looks up. For a second, her eyes meet mine. There is no recognition, no invitation. Just a flicker, like a bird passing in front of the sun. Then she looks away, gathers her things, and leaves. The door closes behind her with a soft thud, and the bell above it chimes a note that lingers in the air like a question.

I think about the words I haven’t written, the pages I haven’t filled. I think about the discipline it takes to sit in front of a blank screen and trust that something will come. Writing is not inspiration; it is excavation. You dig into the dirt of your own mind, and sometimes you find fossils, and sometimes you find only dust. But you keep digging because the alternative is to let the silence win. The alternative is to let the moments slip away ungathered, unmarked, unremembered.

I remember a walk I took last autumn. It was November, and the sky was the colour of old paper. The trees had lost their leaves, and the branches looked like veins against the grey. I was walking through a park, and I saw a bench with a plaque. It said, “In memory of Eleanor, who loved this view.” I sat on the bench and looked at the view. It was a pond with a single swan, a weeping willow, and a path that curved into nothing. I tried to imagine Eleanor. I gave her curly hair and a habit of humming while she walked. I gave her a kindness she may never have had. I made her into a character, because that is what we do. We cannot bear the thought of a life lived entirely unknown. We must turn strangers into stories.

There is a philosophy that says we are all just matter and energy, just atoms arranging themselves into shapes that think they are alive. But sitting in this café, watching the world spin around me like a carousel, I feel something more. I feel the weight of connection, of being a witness. I am here, and I am not here. I am a permanent observer, and I am a passing ghost. The coffee cup is a vessel. The window is a mirror. The stranger is a mirror too, reflecting back the things I have not yet understood about myself.

I think of the mornings I have wasted staring at the ceiling. The nights I have spent in beds that were not my own. The friendships that burned bright and then left only ash. And I think of the people I have loved, how they exist now only as fragments in my memory. A laugh. A gesture. A goodbye that was meant to be temporary but became permanent. We are all collections of these fragments. We are collages of moments and mistakes and small acts of grace.

The café grows louder. A group of teenagers laugh at something on a phone. A couple argues in hushed tones. The barista calls out another name. I have been here for two hours, and I have written nothing and everything. I have filled the page with the invisible ink of attention. I have not written a story, but I have lived one. Every observation is a line of dialogue. Every stranger is a protagonist.

Outside, the light changes. The sun breaks through the clouds, and the street is suddenly golden. The window becomes a painting. The woman in the blue coat is gone, but her ghost remains. The student is gone, but her pen still scratches in my imagination. And I am here, still here, with a cold coffee and a warm heart and a thousand more words waiting to be born.

This is what it means to be alive. To notice. To remember. To sit in a small café by a window that looks onto a street you have never walked, and to walk it anyway, with your eyes.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
